segments.title: Testing a triple-turret Orion concept
segments.summary: A proposed Orion-style battleship uses triple mounts, with a 14-inch game model standing in for 13.5-inch guns.
segments.body: Clarke treats the displayed 14-inch mount as a practical stand-in rather than a claim that the alternative Royal Navy ship would actually carry that calibre. He considers a 13.5-inch weapon a plausible fit inside a mounting designed for a 14-inch gun. The hull otherwise follows the Orion pattern, allowing the viewer to isolate the consequences of replacing twin turrets with triples. He also notes the curious game result that the twin mounts exhausted their ammunition before the triples, while leaving the reason open rather than treating the simulation as historical proof.

evidence.note: Clarke explains the 14-inch stand-in and the triple-turret Orion-style design.

segments.title: A twelve-gun Queen Elizabeth alternative
segments.summary: The central counterfactual is a Queen Elizabeth class with triple 15-inch turrets and a much heavier broadside.
segments.body: Clarke presents a version of Queen Elizabeth carrying twelve 15-inch guns in four triple turrets rather than the historical eight-gun battery. That raises the nominal main-battery firepower by half and makes Warspite a vivid example of the counterfactual. He emphasizes that this is not merely an attractive game model but a practical design path worth testing. The moment establishes why an earlier British triple-turret decision could transform the later class rather than simply alter its silhouette.

evidence.note: Clarke introduces his triple-15-inch Queen Elizabeth design and its added firepower.

segments.title: Why the mystery turret photograph cannot yet be shown
segments.summary: A permission question reveals why a privately supplied naval photograph can start research without yet serving as publicly reproducible proof.
segments.body: Clarke says he requested permission to show the mystery turret photograph but does not expect it to be granted. The image therefore remains a research lead whose original source and provenance must be traced independently. He compares the problem with other privately held naval and biographical records that owners or historians' estates would not release. This exchange is useful for understanding why a credible private clue can guide archival work while remaining unavailable as published evidence.
segments.question: Can the photograph of the possible early triple turret be shown with permission?
segments.answerShort: No permission is likely, so Clarke must trace the photograph's source and corroborate it through accessible records.

evidence.note: Clarke explains the failed permission route and the need to trace a private photograph through other records.

segments.title: Why all-forward gun arrangements appealed
segments.summary: A question about Nelson-style layouts leads to the Royal Navy's long interest in maximizing guns that could fire ahead.
segments.body: Clarke links the attraction of concentrated forward batteries to an older Royal Navy preference for having six guns able to engage ahead. He reaches back to the battle associated with Admiral Tegetthoff and the perceived viability of the steam ram to show the lineage of forward-fire thinking. That preference helps explain Dreadnought's arrangement rather than making the Nelson-style concept an abrupt post-war invention. He adds that, had superfiring been available, Fisher might have preferred as many as eight guns capable of firing forward.
segments.question: Why choose a Nelson-style all-forward arrangement for a triple-turret battleship?
segments.answerShort: It maximizes forward firepower, a preference Clarke connects to long-running Royal Navy thinking and to Dreadnought's design.

evidence.note: Clarke answers the layout question through the value placed on guns firing forward.

segments.title: Would Vickers build a triple turret as a sales prototype?
segments.summary: A reference to Vickers repeatedly offering triple turrets leads to the possibility that a working mounting was built to prove the design to customers.
segments.body: The question recalls Norman Friedman's discussion of Vickers trying to sell the Admiralty a triple turret. Clarke reasons that an active sales campaign would at minimum require plans and engineering work. In this period, moving from drawings to a working example could be the practical way to test the design and persuade a buyer. He treats that logic as a reason to search for corroborating industrial records, not as proof that the photographed mounting is already identified.
segments.question: Could Vickers have built a working triple turret while trying to sell the design to the Admiralty?
segments.answerShort: It is plausible because a working prototype could test and demonstrate a sales design, but archival confirmation is still needed.

evidence.note: The question cites Vickers sales efforts and Clarke explains why a working prototype would be plausible but unconfirmed.

segments.title: Separating machinery from magazines
segments.summary: A hull-size question becomes an argument for placing machinery aft and the main battery and magazines forward.
segments.body: The answer accepts that a triple mount affects beam and enlarges the protected volume required around shell and powder rooms. Clarke argues that a Nelson-like arrangement can partly offset the penalty by putting machinery aft and the main battery and magazines forward. This separation reduces the number of repeated protective and thermal layers needed between engines and ammunition spaces. Supporting facilities can occupy the middle of the hull, creating additional distance between the two principal hazards instead of forcing a simple proportional enlargement everywhere.
segments.question: Would wider triple turrets force a much larger battleship hull?
segments.answerShort: They affect beam, but separating machinery and magazines can simplify the protected internal layout and offset part of the penalty.

evidence.note: Clarke discusses turret width, magazine safety, machinery placement, and protective layering.

segments.title: Speed and oil firing in a radical design
segments.summary: Clarke argues that a navy willing to adopt triple turrets might also accept semi-oil or oil firing to reach a worthwhile speed increase.
segments.body: The discussion contrasts 21-knot, 24-knot, and faster alternatives against boiler numbers, cost, and fleet usefulness. Clarke favors semi-oil or oil firing for the more ambitious designs rather than assuming they must retain an all-coal plant. His counterfactual depends on political as well as technical change: a group able to overcome institutional caution on triple mounts might also win the easier argument for oil. He further notes that boilers, though demanding industrial products, were available from more British yards and were easier to manufacture than heavy-gun turrets.
segments.question: Would a triple-turret battleship need too many boilers to make a useful speed gain?
segments.answerShort: Clarke sees boiler output as less limiting than turret manufacture and thinks the same reform coalition could make semi-oil or oil firing plausible.

evidence.note: Clarke compares boiler numbers, speed, and the case for oil-fired machinery.

segments.title: Would extra speed change fleet tactics?
segments.summary: A challenge to the value of 24 knots separates the strategic benefits of faster ships from what the Grand Fleet could exploit tactically in formation.
segments.body: The question argues that extra speed is wasted when new battleships must operate with a slower battle line and may not change Jellicoe's torpedo-avoidance decision. Clarke agrees that there is a larger discussion about the Grand Fleet's tactical maturity and does not claim different ships automatically produce a different Jutland decision. His design case is narrower: the 24-knot variant is the smallest version he considers to offer a meaningful speed advantage while retaining an armour advantage. The exchange shows why a faster battleship can improve deployment and force-level choices without guaranteeing a different maneuver in a specific battle.
segments.question: Would 24-knot battleships gain anything if they still operated with slower dreadnoughts, and would they change Jellicoe's decision at Jutland?
segments.answerShort: Their main benefit may be strategic rather than tactical, and Clarke does not assume extra speed alone would change Jellicoe's torpedo response.

evidence.note: The exchange distinguishes strategic speed benefits, fleet formation limits, Jellicoe's choice, and the retained armour advantage.

segments.title: What makes a fast battleship?
segments.summary: The alternative design tests how much speed is needed before a powerful dreadnought becomes a true fast battleship.
segments.body: Clarke distinguishes a conservative 24-knot improvement from the much more ambitious speeds associated with older battlecruisers. He compares the proposal with Nelson's 23 knots on 45,000 shaft horsepower to argue that 50,000 shaft horsepower can support the slightly faster alternative despite its beam. The 24-knot target also repeats the three-knot jump from pre-dreadnoughts to Dreadnought, making it a deliberately conservative step. In Clarke's stricter terminology, however, only a ship capable of about 28 knots qualifies as a true fast battleship rather than merely a faster super-dreadnought.

evidence.note: Clarke explains the proposed speed range and his definition of a fast battleship.

segments.title: How a triple-turret tradition could persist
segments.summary: A pre-war British commitment to triple mounts could influence later choices between triples and quadruples on capital ships.
segments.body: The question asks whether an earlier triple-turret decision would more naturally produce a Queen Elizabeth with triple 13.5-inch guns. Clarke instead expects the class to retain the 15-inch calibre, perhaps with twelve guns rather than eight, because its purpose was to advance beyond the preceding generation. A conventional four-triple Queen Elizabeth could in turn make a similar battery attractive for the slower Revenge class. Decades of operating triples would also change the later choice between quadruple mounts and triple 16-inch guns for the King George V generation.
segments.question: Would an early Royal Navy triple turret lead to a Queen Elizabeth class armed with triple 13.5-inch guns?
segments.answerShort: Clarke expects Queen Elizabeth still to adopt 15-inch guns, potentially twelve of them, with the experience then shaping the Revenge and later King George V designs.

evidence.note: Clarke discusses triple-turret standardization, later capital ships, and the impact of a stronger Queen Elizabeth.

segments.title: How a twelve-gun fast Queen Elizabeth could reset the arms race
segments.summary: A 28-knot Queen Elizabeth with twelve 15-inch guns would outclass contemporary battle lines and intensify pressure for larger American and British capital ships.
segments.body: Clarke imagines a 28-knot Queen Elizabeth carrying twelve 15-inch guns, a combination that contemporary fleets could not match directly. Even a later reduction to nine 15-inch guns would leave the Royal Navy with an unusually fast battle line, while battlecruiser designs such as Renown could trade a smaller main battery for still more speed. The discussion notes that some United States Navy officers already viewed the Standard-type battleships as threatened by Queen Elizabeth and Hood. The counterfactual therefore magnifies an existing design pressure rather than inventing a naval arms race from nothing.

evidence.note: The discussion follows the effects of a fast twelve-gun Queen Elizabeth into the Royal Navy battle line, battlecruisers, and American design pressure.

segments.title: Can a destroyer disrupt a torpedo with its bow wave?
segments.summary: A brief question asks whether a ship's bow wave could upset a torpedo's guidance, especially as torpedoes improved.
segments.body: The question distinguishes early torpedoes from later weapons whose gyroscopes and pendulums might better withstand a sudden hydrodynamic disturbance. Clarke does not offer a firm technical history of the guidance mechanism or evidence of controlled Royal Navy trials. He does, however, note that destroyers attempted to use their bow waves this way in both world wars. Most survived, making the exchange a cautious pointer to how evasive seamanship could test a torpedo's behavior under combat conditions.
segments.question: Could a destroyer's bow wave deflect or disrupt an incoming torpedo?
segments.answerShort: Clarke is unsure of the detailed mechanism, but says destroyers tried it in both world wars and most survived.

evidence.note: Clarke responds to the bow-wave question with a cautious wartime example.

segments.title: Why the mystery turret looks more like 14-inch than 12-inch
segments.summary: A Japanese export-design possibility prompts Clarke to separate his confidence in the photographed gun calibre from his much weaker confidence in the written 1909 date.
segments.body: The question connects Vickers' Japanese work with Fuso and Kongo proposals and asks whether the mystery image could show an offered triple mounting. Clarke says the photograph is marked Vickers and carries the number 1909, while accompanying notes identify the weapon as 14-inch. He also judges the barrel proportions and shape closer to a 14-inch gun than to a 12-inch gun, unless the latter had been very heavily rebuilt. His conclusion is deliberately uneven: he is more confident about the 14-inch identification than about the date and still requires archival corroboration.
segments.question: Could the photographed triple turret be a Vickers mounting offered for a Japanese battleship, and how certain are its calibre and date?
segments.answerShort: A Japanese connection is possible, and Clarke is more confident that the guns are 14-inch than that the handwritten 1909 date is reliable.

evidence.note: The exchange connects Japanese proposals to the photograph and separates confidence in the 14-inch identification from confidence in the date.

segments.title: Why larger-calibre guns rapidly lengthen a battleship layout
segments.summary: A Fuso-class example shows how the calibre-length convention makes a 14-inch gun substantially longer than a 12-inch weapon and complicates multi-turret hull arrangements.
segments.body: The comment notes that Fuso needed considerable length to accommodate six twin 14-inch turrets, suggesting that the mounting choice was deliberate. Clarke explains that a gun described as 40 calibres long measures forty times its bore diameter. A 40-calibre 12-inch gun is about forty feet long, while a 14-inch weapon of the same relative length adds roughly six feet eight inches. The difference grows further with 45- or 50-calibre weapons, so changing calibre affects turret and hull geometry rather than simply shell diameter.

evidence.note: Clarke uses the Fuso discussion and the calibre-length convention to show how 14-inch guns demand appreciably more length than 12-inch guns.

segments.title: Triple turrets in the 1909 arms race
segments.summary: A question about 1909 places British triple-turret choices alongside Italian, Russian, Japanese, American, Austrian, and German battleship programs.
segments.body: Clarke stresses that Britain would not be introducing triple mounts into an empty international field. Italy and Russia were already working on triple-turret battleships, Austria was pursuing similar ideas, and Vickers had offered triples for Japanese projects while the United States and France developed their own new dreadnoughts. Germany is the most consequential comparison because its contemporary wing-turret ships could bring only eight guns to a broadside despite carrying six turrets. A British Orion with larger guns and a thirteen-gun broadside would therefore sharpen the competitive pressure on the Imperial German Navy even if triple mounts themselves were not uniquely British.
segments.question: What were other navies doing with dreadnoughts in 1909, and would a British triple turret matter?
segments.answerShort: Several navies were already pursuing triple mounts, but an early British move could produce an especially large broadside advantage over Germany.

evidence.note: Clarke surveys contemporary foreign programs and compares potential British and German broadsides.

segments.title: The muzzle-blast problem in triple turrets
segments.summary: Closely spaced barrels raise a practical question about muzzle blast, shell dispersion, and delayed firing circuits.
segments.body: The exchange identifies barrel spacing as a cost and hull-size issue rather than merely a question of adding a third gun. Preserving twin-turret spacing would demand a wider mounting, a larger barbette, more internal protection, and potentially a larger ship. Bringing the barrels closer together instead risks muzzle-blast interference and poor shell dispersion, problems the Royal Navy had already considered while developing multi-gun secondary mounts. The cited American solution of firing the guns fractions of a second apart shows how a compact triple mounting could require a new firing circuit and different turret practice.
segments.question: Could close barrel spacing make a triple turret inaccurate?
segments.answerShort: Yes. Blast interference and shell dispersion were real design concerns, and staggered firing was one way navies sought to reduce them.

evidence.note: The comments and response cover barrel spacing, dispersion, and delayed firing.

segments.title: Would Germany match British triple turrets?
segments.summary: Clarke expects a German response to British triple mounts, but not an equally rapid one because shipbuilding capacity matters.
segments.body: The answer treats turret policy as an industrial problem as well as a tactical decision. Clarke expects Germany to follow a successful British triple-turret program because the change would affect the naval competition directly. Britain, however, has the infrastructure, production capacity, and naval funding to switch heavy-mount production more rapidly. Germany's likely delay means identical design intent would not produce identical fleet results on the same timetable.
segments.question: If Britain adopted triple turrets, would Germany immediately do the same?
segments.answerShort: Germany might follow, but Clarke expects Britain to move faster because it had greater industrial capacity and naval funding.

evidence.note: Clarke compares British and German industrial capacity for changing turret production.

segments.title: Why the alternative design has a lightly armoured conning tower
segments.summary: A query about whether the conning-tower armour figure is a mistake leads to Clarke's deliberate choice to spend little protection weight there.
segments.body: The question asks whether the design's roughly two-inch conning-tower protection should instead be a much larger millimetre figure. Clarke says the light value is intentional because he regards heavily armoured conning towers as a poor use of armour weight. He links that view to Royal Navy practice in which admirals often preferred other command positions and conning towers could become storage spaces. The answer exposes a specific protection trade-off inside the larger counterfactual rather than treating every historical armour feature as mandatory.
segments.question: Was the very light conning-tower armour an error in the alternative battleship design?
segments.answerShort: No. Clarke deliberately minimizes it because he considers heavy conning-tower armour a poor use of protection weight.

evidence.note: Clarke confirms the light conning-tower protection is deliberate and explains his view of its limited value.

segments.title: Following the Vickers trail to Barrow-in-Furness
segments.summary: A former shipyard worker points to Barrow-in-Furness archives and the Walney Island gun range as places where the mystery turret may have left records.
segments.body: The suggestion directs Clarke to records connected with the Barrow-in-Furness shipyard and the nearby range where 14-inch guns may have been tested. It also raises the local museum and BAE Systems as possible custodians or guides to surviving material. Clarke confirms that Barrow is the archive and range connection he had in mind. The exchange turns a general Vickers research question into a concrete geographical and institutional trail.
segments.question: Could Barrow-in-Furness shipyard and range records help identify the possible Vickers triple turret?
segments.answerShort: Yes. Clarke identifies Barrow and its associated archives as a promising route and may need to contact the museum or BAE Systems.

evidence.note: The exchange identifies Barrow-in-Furness, the gun range, museum, BAE Systems, and local archives as research leads.

segments.title: Could a Vickers 14-inch turret take Royal Navy 13.5-inch guns?
segments.summary: A concern that adopting the Vickers mounting would force the Royal Navy to accept the firm's 14-inch gun leads to a proposed 13.5-inch substitution.
segments.body: The question notes the Royal Navy's reluctance to surrender control over gun development by adopting a Vickers 14-inch weapon. Clarke separates the gun from the mounting and argues that a turret sized for 14-inch pieces should also accommodate the slightly smaller 13.5-inch Royal Navy gun. Lower weight and diameter might even leave useful margin for movement or elevation inside the mounting. He also emphasizes that Vickers' willingness to sell designs made a Royal Navy adaptation commercially plausible, though the exact engineering still requires evidence.
segments.question: Would using a Vickers triple turret force the Royal Navy to adopt the company's 14-inch gun?
segments.answerShort: Not necessarily. Clarke thinks the larger mounting could accept Royal Navy 13.5-inch guns and might even provide extra elevation margin.

evidence.note: Clarke distinguishes the Vickers mounting from its gun and argues that a 13.5-inch weapon could fit the 14-inch design.

segments.title: Could early triple-turret ships be re-gunned later?
segments.summary: The larger barbette and mounting implied by an early triple turret may create room to replace three smaller guns with two larger weapons during modernization.
segments.body: The question compares triple-turret conservatism with later quadruple-mount reliability problems and asks whether the larger installation creates an upgrade path. Clarke judges that a triple 13.5-inch mounting could plausibly be rebuilt for twin 15-inch guns. A triple 14-inch mounting might even accept twin 16-inch guns if a navy considered the conversion worthwhile. The answer shows that unused volume and weight capacity in an early turret system could affect a ship's modernization potential as well as its original armament.
segments.question: Could a battleship built with triple 13.5- or 14-inch turrets later receive twin 15- or 16-inch guns?
segments.answerShort: Clarke considers both conversions plausible in principle, with the original larger mounting providing room for heavier twin guns.

evidence.note: The exchange considers rebuilding triple 13.5- or 14-inch installations for twin 15- or 16-inch guns.

segments.title: Redundancy versus compact triple mounts
segments.summary: A final design question weighs independently mounted guns against a smaller triple turret with heavier external armour and backup systems.
segments.body: The question contrasts Nelson's mechanically linked guns with a larger turret in which each weapon has its own protected machinery. Clarke frames the choice as a trade-off between internal independence and a compact protected enclosure. A smaller turret occupies less deck and barbette volume and can leave weight and space for thicker external armour. Designers can also add secondary and auxiliary systems so that damage to the normal mechanism does not automatically immobilize every gun.
segments.question: Would shared machinery make a triple turret too vulnerable to a single hit?
segments.answerShort: A compact mount trades some independence for space and armour, and can still include auxiliary systems to preserve a degree of redundancy.

evidence.note: Clarke compares shared machinery, turret size, armour, and auxiliary systems.

segments.title: Where to look for early triple-turret evidence
segments.summary: Possible Vickers, Armstrong, Russian, and Japanese design records offer routes for testing the historical basis of early triple mounts.
segments.body: Clarke closes by distinguishing the known Vickers image from the wider archival question. A comment points to Armstrong triple-turret variations for Japan dating as early as 1907, suggesting more than one British firm may have explored the arrangement. Russian records may also preserve evidence because Vickers 14-inch guns were considered for the Borodino or Izmail-class battlecruisers in triple mounts. If those export and foreign-order records survive, they may reveal how far British triple-turret engineering progressed even when the Royal Navy did not adopt it.

evidence.note: Clarke points to Vickers, Armstrong, Russian, and Japanese archival possibilities.
